title: Adiabatic Quantum Phase Estimation
abstract: Quantum phase estimation (QPE) is a central algorithmic primitive that estimates eigenvalues of a Hamiltonian up to precision $ε$ in Heisenberg-limited time $T=Θ(1/ε)$. Standard gate-based implementations of QPE require deep controlled time-evolution circuits and are not native to analog hardware. Here, we present a simple adiabatic protocol for QPE that achieves (up to logarithmic factors) the optimal Heisenberg-limited scaling $T = O\left( \frac{1}ε \log\left(δ^{-1}\right)\right)$ in both the precision $ε$ and failure probability $δ$. By encoding eigenvalues in populations of computational basis states rather than complex phases, our approach is naturally robust against certain dephasing errors. The adiabatic protocol only requires the ability to couple a single ancilla qubit to the system Hamiltonian as well as pairwise couplings within the ancilla register.
summary: This paper introduces an adiabatic protocol for Quantum Phase Estimation that achieves optimal Heisenberg-limited scaling. By using a specialized 'superadiabatic' scheduling technique, the authors suppress errors exponentially, making adiabatic hardware a viable platform for high-precision phase estimation.
main_problem: Developing an adiabatic version of Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) that achieves optimal Heisenberg-limited scaling in both precision and failure probability while avoiding the deep circuits required by gate-based methods.
main_result: The authors present a superadiabatic adiabatic protocol using a Denjoy-Carleman clock that achieves near-exponential suppression of diabatic leakage, resulting in a total runtime of O(1/epsilon * log(1/delta)).
method: The protocol uses a multi-stage adiabatic evolution with a reparameterized Roland-Cerf schedule and a non-quasianalytic Denjoy-Carleman clock to ensure vanishing derivatives at the boundaries of each stage.
model_or_system: An n-qubit system with Hamiltonian Hs coupled to an m-qubit ancilla register, utilizing transverse fields, pairwise ZiZj interactions, and a coupling between the system energy and a single ancilla qubit.
key_observables: Diabatic leakage (transition probability), precision (epsilon), and failure probability (delta).
important_parameters: Precision epsilon, failure probability delta, adiabatic parameter A, and the number of stages m.
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian must be smooth (C-infinity) with vanishing derivatives at the endpoints, and the eigenvalues must remain separated by a minimum energy gap.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares linear, Roland-Cerf, and superadiabatic schedules; Figure 2 shows numerical evidence that superadiabatic schedules suppress transitions exponentially compared to power-law decay in standard schedules.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of adiabatic QPE, defines the multi-stage Hamiltonian model, develops the mathematical framework for superadiabatic estimates using Denjoy-Carleman clocks, proves leakage bounds and runtime complexity, and demonstrates applications to adiabatic Grover search.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum computing and open quantum systems as it provides a way to implement fundamental algorithms on analog/adiabatic hardware with optimal scaling and robustness to dephasing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A visualization of three different schedule profiles: (a) The linear ramp profile s(t) = t/T. (b) The Roland- Cerf schedule (10) mirroring the behavior of the spectral gap. (c) The Roland-Cerf schedule reparametrized by a DC clock, which we call superadiabatic RC. Also shown is the behavior of the DC clock function, whose endpoint derivatives van- ish and which thereby near-exponentially suppresses diabatic transitions.

FIG. 2. Numerical computation of the diabatic leakage via exact diagonalization of the two-level Hamiltonian (8). Four different schedules are considered: linear, Roland-Cerf, su- peradiabatic Roland-Cerf, and the DC clock function, which can be interpreted as the linear schedule reparametrized by the DC clock. While the linear and Roland-Cerf schedules yield a power-law decay, the reparametrized schedules sup- press diabatic transitions near-exponentially. Both RC-type schedules display St¨uckelberg oscillations arising from coher- ent interference between nonadiabatic transitions.
